01.q: A qubit is in the state $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}|0\rangle + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}|1\rangle$. What does a single measurement give you?
options: Both 0 and 1 simultaneously
0 or 1, each with probability 1/2 — and the state collapses to that outcome
The value 0.5
The two amplitudes themselves
01.explain: One shot yields exactly one classical outcome, sampled by the Born rule ($P = |\text{amplitude}|^2$). The 'both at once' slogan confuses the pre-measurement amplitudes with the post-measurement result.

01.q: Which of these is a valid qubit state?
options: $0.5|0\rangle + 0.5|1\rangle$
$|0\rangle + |1\rangle$
$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}|0\rangle + \frac{1}{2}|1\rangle$
$0.75|0\rangle + 0.25|1\rangle$
01.explain: Normalization lives on the squares of the amplitudes: $(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2})^2 + (\frac{1}{2})^2 = \frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4} = 1$. The others sum to 1/2, 2, and 0.625 — physics forbids all three.

01.q: You run $H|0\rangle$ for 1024 shots and get 498 zeros, 526 ones. What should you conclude?
options: The circuit is wrong — it should give exactly 512/512
The simulator is broken
This is consistent with $P(0)=P(1)=1/2$ — counts fluctuate like coin flips ($\sigma \approx 16$ here)
The qubit decohered
01.explain: Each shot is an independent Born-rule sample. Deviations of a few standard deviations ($\sqrt{N \cdot p \cdot (1-p)} \approx 16$ shots) are expected; that's why we judge distributions statistically, not exactly.

01.q: You want a state with $P(1) = 1/4$ starting from $|0\rangle$. Which gate does it?
options: H, because it creates superposition
$RY(\pi/3)$, because $\sin^2(\pi/6) = 1/4$
$RY(\pi/4)$, because a quarter turn gives a quarter probability
X, then measure quickly
01.explain: $RY(\theta)|0\rangle = \cos(\theta/2)|0\rangle + \sin(\theta/2)|1\rangle$, so $P(1) = \sin^2(\theta/2) = 1/4$ needs $\theta/2 = \pi/6$, i.e. $\theta = \pi/3$ ($60^\circ$). Angles map to probabilities through $\sin^2$ of the HALF-angle — the widget's slider showed you this curve.

02.q: You apply the $Z$ gate to the fair state $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}|0\rangle + \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}|1\rangle$. What happens to the measurement probabilities $P(0)$ and $P(1)$?
options: They swap: $P(0) \leftrightarrow P(1)$
Nothing — both stay $1/2$; $Z$ flips the phase of $|1\rangle$, not the odds
Both become 0
$P(1)$ doubles
02.explain: $Z$ sends $|1\rangle \to -|1\rangle$, so the state becomes $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}|0\rangle - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}|1\rangle = |-\rangle$. Probabilities are the squared magnitudes, and $|{-}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}|^2 = 1/2$ — unchanged. Only the hidden sign (phase) moved, and that is exactly what powers interference later.

02.q: Starting from $|0\rangle$, is 'H then Z' the same circuit as 'Z then H'?
options: Yes — they use the same two gates
H-then-Z gives $|-\rangle$, but Z-then-H gives $|+\rangle$ — order matters
Both give $|+\rangle$
The order never matters for quantum gates
02.explain: $H|0\rangle = |+\rangle$, then $Z|+\rangle = |-\rangle$. But $Z|0\rangle = |0\rangle$, then $H|0\rangle = |+\rangle$. Same ingredients, different results: quantum gates generally do not commute, so order is part of the circuit.

02.q: You apply $H$, then $Z$, then $H$ (in that order) to one qubit. What single gate does this sequence equal?
options: $Z$ again — nothing really changed
$X$, the bit-flip
The identity (it does nothing)
02.explain: Sandwiching the phase-flip $Z$ between two Hadamards turns it into the bit-flip: $H \cdot Z \cdot H = X$. The Hadamards rotate the frame so that a 'phase' change becomes a visible 'bit' change — your first glimpse of why hidden phases matter.

02.q: In Qiskit you build a 2-qubit circuit, apply $X$ to qubit 0, and measure. Which bitstring appears?
options: '10', because qubit 0 is written first
'01', because Qiskit puts qubit 0 on the right (little-endian)
02.explain: Qiskit is little-endian: qubit 0 is the rightmost character of the bitstring. Flipping qubit 0 lights up that rightmost bit, giving '01'. Read Qiskit bitstrings right-to-left and this trap disappears.

03.q: You prepare the Bell pair $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|00\rangle + |11\rangle)$ and measure both qubits many times. Which outcomes appear?
options: All four (00, 01, 10, 11), each about 25%
Only 00 and 11, each about 50% — the qubits always agree
Only 00 — the qubits are frozen
Only 01 and 10 — the qubits always disagree
03.explain: $|\Phi^+\rangle$ has amplitude only on $|00\rangle$ and $|11\rangle$, so a mismatch is impossible: you get 00 or 11, about half each. Each qubit measured alone is still a fair 50/50 — it is the *link between them* that is certain, not either coin.

options: Both qubits are in superposition at the same time
The state cannot be written as 'qubit A does X' and separately 'qubit B does Y' — only the pair has a description
The two qubits are physically close together
Measuring one qubit sends a signal to the other
03.explain: Entanglement is un-factorability. A product state like $|+\rangle \otimes |+\rangle$ splits into two independent stories; $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|00\rangle+|11\rangle)$ does not — no description of A alone plus B alone reproduces it. Superposition and proximity are not the point; un-splittability is.

03.q: The CNOT gate has a control qubit and a target qubit. What does it do?
options: It always flips both qubits
It flips the target only when the control is $|1\rangle$; otherwise it does nothing
It measures the control qubit
It swaps the two qubits
03.explain: CNOT = 'controlled-NOT': if the control is $|1\rangle$, flip the target; if the control is $|0\rangle$, leave it alone. Apply it to a control that is in superposition (via H first) and the 'flip / don't flip' gets tied to the control's coin-flip — that knot is exactly what creates entanglement.

03.q: Alice and Bob each hold one qubit of a Bell pair, far apart. Alice measures hers and gets 1. Can she use this to send Bob a message faster than light?
options: Yes — Bob's qubit instantly becomes 1, so information travelled
No — Bob's results look completely random until the two compare notes over an ordinary (light-speed-limited) channel
Yes, but only if they measured at exactly the same time
No, because the qubits were never really linked
03.explain: Alice learns Bob's value, but learning is not sending. On its own, Bob's stream of results is perfectly random 50/50 — nothing Alice does is detectable on his side. The correlation only becomes visible when the two result lists are laid side by side, which requires a normal message. Real link, no telephone: entanglement never carries a signal.

04.q: The $Z$ observable is a scoreboard: it scores the outcome $0$ as $+1$ and the outcome $1$ as $-1$. A qubit is in the state $|0\rangle$. What energy does $Z$ report for it?
options: $0$, because the qubit is labelled $|0\rangle$
$+1$ — $Z$ assigns the score $+1$ to outcome $0$
$-1$, because $0$ is the 'off' state
It is undefined until you measure
04.explain: $Z$ is a lookup table, not the bit value: outcome $0 \to +1$, outcome $1 \to -1$. A qubit that always reads $0$ always scores $+1$, so its expectation value (energy) is $\langle Z\rangle = +1$. Watch the sign — the label $0$ maps to the score $+1$, not $0$.

04.q: For a fair qubit $|+\rangle = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|0\rangle+|1\rangle)$, every single measurement returns $+1$ or $-1$. What is the expectation value $\langle Z\rangle$?
options: Impossible to define — you can never actually measure $0$
$0$ — the long-run average of half $+1$ and half $-1$
$+1$ — measurement rounds up
$+0.5$ — halfway to certain
04.explain: $\langle Z\rangle = (+1)P(0) + (-1)P(1) = \tfrac12 - \tfrac12 = 0$. No single shot ever reads $0$, yet the *average* of many shots does — the same predict-the-statistics move from Basecamp 1, now reading energy instead of counting heads.

04.q: You build the state $RY(\pi/3)|0\rangle$, the 75/25 biased coin ($P(0)=\tfrac34$, $P(1)=\tfrac14$). What is its energy $\langle Z\rangle$?
options: $0$, because it is in superposition
$+1$, because green wins
04.explain: $\langle Z\rangle = P(0)\cdot(+1) + P(1)\cdot(-1) = \tfrac34 - \tfrac14 = +0.5$. It is a weighted average of the two scores, not just $P(0)$. Superposition does not force the energy to $0$ — only a *fair* coin does that.

04.q: The $ZZ$ Hamiltonian scores a two-qubit outcome $+1$ when the qubits agree (00 or 11) and $-1$ when they disagree (01 or 10). What is $\langle ZZ\rangle$ for the Bell pair $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(|00\rangle+|11\rangle)$?
options: $0$, because each qubit alone is a fair 50/50
$+1$ — the pair always agrees, so every shot scores $+1$
$-1$ — entangled qubits always disagree
$+0.5$, halfway between agree and disagree
04.explain: $\Phi^+$ only ever gives 00 or 11 — agreement on every shot — so every score is $+1$ and $\langle ZZ\rangle = +1$. $\langle ZZ\rangle$ scores the *pair*, not either coin (each qubit alone is still fair). Flip one qubit to get $\Psi^+$ and they always disagree instead: $\langle ZZ\rangle = -1$. This 'agreement energy' is the seed of the cost Hamiltonians you'll minimize at the summit.

05.q: You want the lowest-energy state of a Hamiltonian but don't know which state it is. What is the variational strategy?
options: Measure $|0\rangle$ once and accept whatever comes out
Build a state with a tunable knob $\theta$, read its energy, and adjust $\theta$ to push the energy down
Solve the Schrödinger equation by hand for every possible state
Pick a random state and hope it is the ground state
05.explain: A **parameterized circuit** gives you a dial. You prepare $|\psi(\theta)\rangle$, measure its energy $\langle H\rangle$, then nudge $\theta$ to lower that energy and repeat — descending the landscape toward the ground state instead of guessing or brute-forcing.

05.q: In the variational loop the quantum computer prepares $|\psi(\theta)\rangle$ and reports its energy. Who decides the next $\theta$ to try?
options: The quantum computer, by measuring more shots
A classical optimizer (e.g. COBYLA) running on an ordinary computer
The Hamiltonian chooses it automatically
It is fixed at the start and never changes
05.explain: It is a **hybrid** loop. Quantum hardware is the energy meter — it evaluates $\langle H\rangle$ at a given $\theta$, which is the hard part. A plain classical optimizer reads those energies and picks the next $\theta$, walking downhill until it reaches the valley floor.

05.q: For the ansatz $RY(\theta)|0\rangle$ and cost Hamiltonian $H = Z + 0.5\,X$, the energy is $E(\theta) = \cos\theta + 0.5\sin\theta$. What is $E(0)$ (the state $|0\rangle$)?
05.explain: $E(0) = \cos 0 + 0.5\sin 0 = 1 + 0 = +1$. Starting at $|0\rangle$ sits you high on the hillside; the whole job is to turn the knob until the energy drops to the bottom of the valley.

05.q: Your optimizer keeps lowering the energy and stops at $E = -1.118$, unable to go lower. What have you found, and why isn't the ground state just $|1\rangle$?
options: A bug — energy can never be negative
The ground state; for $H = Z + 0.5X$ it is a tilted mix of $|0\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$, not a basis state
The state $|1\rangle$, which is always the ground state
The highest-energy state
05.explain: $-1.118 = -\sqrt{1.25}$ is the smallest eigenvalue of $Z + 0.5X$ — the **ground energy**. Because the $X$ term tilts the Hamiltonian, the lowest-energy state is a rotated blend, not $|1\rangle$. That is exactly why you had to *search* with a tunable angle instead of reading off an obvious answer — and for a cost Hamiltonian, that bottom encodes the solution to your problem.

06.q: In Max-Cut you split a graph's nodes into two groups. Which edges count toward your score?
options: Edges with both endpoints in the same group
Edges whose endpoints land in different groups (they cross the divide)
Every edge in the graph
Only edges connected to node 0
06.explain: A **cut** edge crosses between the two groups. Max-Cut asks for the split that severs the *most* edges. On the 4-node ring the best you can do is the checkerboard, cutting all 4 — an edge with both ends in one group scores nothing.

06.q: Each node gets a qubit and we use the cost $H_C = \sum_{\text{edges}} Z_iZ_j$, where $Z_iZ_j$ scores $+1$ when the two qubits agree and $-1$ when they disagree. Why does minimizing $\langle H_C\rangle$ solve Max-Cut?
options: It doesn't — minimizing agreement would ruin the cut
A cut edge means the qubits disagree, scoring $-1$, so the lowest total energy has the most cut edges
Minimizing $\langle H_C\rangle$ always selects the state $|0000\rangle$
Because $H_C$ counts the number of nodes, not edges
06.explain: This is the whole encoding, straight from Basecamp 4: a cut edge = two nodes in different groups = qubits disagree = score $-1$. More cuts means lower energy, so the ground state of $H_C$ is the maximum cut. 'Solve Max-Cut' becomes 'find the ground state' — and that's the variational job from Basecamp 5. ($|0000\rangle$ cuts nothing and sits at the *highest* energy, $+4$.)

06.q: A QAOA layer has a cost unitary (built from the edges) and a mixer unitary (RX rotations on every qubit). What goes wrong if you drop the mixer?
options: Nothing — the cost unitary alone finds the answer
The cost unitary only adds phases to $|+\rangle^{\otimes n}$, so the measured bitstrings stay uniformly random — the mixer is what turns those phases into a real preference
The circuit becomes invalid and cannot run
It measures the wrong number of qubits
06.explain: The cost unitary is diagonal: on $|+\rangle^{\otimes n}$ it stamps a phase on each configuration but leaves every measurement probability equal. Phases are invisible to a Z-measurement until the **mixer** rotates them into amplitude differences (interference). Cost *marks* the good cuts; the mixer *makes them likely*. You need both — this is why QAOA alternates the two.

06.q: Your optimized QAOA circuit is sampled 2048 times and the most frequent bitstring is 0101. What does the approximation ratio being $1.0$ tell you?
options: The circuit used exactly one layer
Your solution cuts as many edges as the true maximum — it's an optimal cut
The optimizer failed and returned a random guess
Exactly 1 out of every 2048 shots was useful
06.explain: The approximation ratio is (edges your solution cuts) ÷ (edges the best possible solution cuts). A ratio of $1.0$ means you matched the true maximum — here 0101 is the checkerboard, cutting all 4 edges. You just solved a real optimization problem end to end with QAOA. 🏔️ Summit reached.
